questions.question: 中文导读：What does temperature < 1.0 do to a language model's output distribution?
options: 中文选项：Makes the distribution more uniform (more random)
中文选项：Sharpens the distribution, making the highest-probability token more likely
中文选项：Removes all tokens except the top one
中文选项：Has no effect on the output
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Temperature < 1.0 divides logits by a number less than 1, which amplifies differences between logits. After softmax, the highest-probability token gets an even larger share. Temperature approaches 0 gives greedy (argmax) decoding.

questions.question: 中文导读：What is the key difference between top-k and top-p (nucleus) sampling?
options: 中文选项：Top-k is faster than top-p
中文选项：Top-k keeps a fixed number of tokens; top-p keeps a variable number based on cumulative probability
中文选项：Top-p only works with temperature = 1.0
中文选项：Top-k works on logits while top-p works on probabilities
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Top-k always keeps exactly k tokens regardless of the probability distribution. Top-p adaptively keeps the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds p. When the model is confident, top-p keeps few tokens; when uncertain, it keeps many.

questions.question: 中文导读：Why can't you backpropagate through a standard sampling operation z ~ N(mu, sigma^2)?
options: 中文选项：Normal distributions don't have gradients
中文选项：The sampling operation is non-deterministic and has no well-defined derivative with respect to mu and sigma
中文选项：PyTorch doesn't support normal distributions
中文选项：The gradient is always exactly zero
questions.explanation: 中文解析：Sampling introduces a stochastic discontinuity — you can't compute d(sample)/d(mu) for a random draw. The reparameterization trick solves this by writing z = mu + sigma * epsilon (where epsilon ~ N(0,1)), making z a deterministic, differentiable function of mu and sigma.

questions.question: 中文导读：In Metropolis-Hastings MCMC, what happens if the proposal standard deviation is set much too large?
options: 中文选项：The chain converges faster because it takes bigger steps
中文选项：Most proposals land in low-probability regions and are rejected, so the chain barely moves
中文选项：The stationary distribution changes to a uniform distribution
中文选项：The burn-in period becomes zero
questions.explanation: 中文解析：With a large proposal standard deviation, proposed points are far from the current position and likely land in low-probability regions. These are rejected, causing the chain to stay stuck at the current point. The optimal acceptance rate is about 23% for high-dimensional Gaussian proposals.

questions.question: 中文导读：In rejection sampling, what happens to the acceptance rate as the dimensionality of the target distribution increases?
options: 中文选项：It stays constant regardless of dimension
中文选项：It increases because there are more dimensions to accept in
中文选项：It drops exponentially because most of the proposal volume gets rejected
中文选项：It approaches 50% in all cases
questions.explanation: 中文解析：In high dimensions, the volume of the proposal distribution that overlaps with the target distribution shrinks exponentially. The bound M grows, and the acceptance rate (1/M) drops exponentially. This is the curse of dimensionality for rejection sampling.
